m_Item1.QuestionText: What is overfitting in the context of machine learning?

Options.OptionText: When the model performs well on training data but poorly on unseen data.
When the model has high bias and low variance.
When the model performs equally well on both training and test data.
When the model has high variance and low bias.

m_Item1.Explanation: In overfitting model memorises the datapoints and fails on new data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a common method to reduce overfitting in machine learning models?

Options.OptionText: Adding more layers to the neural network.
Increasing the learning rate during training.
Adding dropout regularization.
Decreasing the size of the training dataset.

m_Item1.Explanation: Overfitting can be avoided by dropping some neurons randomly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does underfitting mean in the context of machine learning?

Options.OptionText: The model performs well on training data but poorly on unseen data.
The model is too complex and fits the noise in the training data.
The model is not able to capture the underlying patterns of the data.
The model has high variance and low bias.

m_Item1.Explanation: Underfitting is the case where model fails to learn from training data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a characteristic of an underfitted model?

Options.OptionText: High accuracy on training data and low accuracy on test data.
Low accuracy on both training and test data.
Low accuracy on training data and high accuracy on test data.
High accuracy on both training and test data.

m_Item1.Explanation: The performance is worst on both training and testing data in case of underfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which technique can help in identifying if a model is overfitting or underfitting?

Options.OptionText: Cross-validation.
Using a large number of epochs during training.
Adding more features to the dataset.
Increasing the batch size during training.

m_Item1.Explanation: Cross validation is a smppling technique used to address the problem of overfitting or underfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a symptom of overfitting in a machine learning model?

Options.OptionText: Low training error and low test error.
High training error and low test error.
High training error and high test error.
Low training error and high test error.

m_Item1.Explanation: In overfitting model memorises the datapoints and fails on new data leading to low training error and high test error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which regularization technique penalizes large coefficients in the model to prevent overfitting?

Options.OptionText: L1 regularization (Lasso)
L2 regularization (Ridge)
Dropout regularization
Elastic Net regularization

m_Item1.Explanation: L2 regularization(Ridge) adds the square of the coefficient multiplied by learning rate to the cost function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of decision trees, what can lead to overfitting?

Options.OptionText: Increasing the number of trees in the forest
Setting the maximum depth of the tree too low
Pruning the tree aggressively
Allowing the tree to grow until each leaf node is pure

m_Item1.Explanation: If the model is allowed to grow till the node is pure, it may lead to overfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a common indication that a machine learning model is overfitting?

Options.OptionText: High training accuracy and low test accuracy
Low training accuracy and high test accuracy
Both training and test accuracies are high
Both training and test accuracies are low

m_Item1.Explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too well, including the noise. This results in high training accuracy but low test accuracy because the model does not generalize well to unseen data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following techniques can be used to reduce overfitting?

Options.OptionText: Removing cross-validation
Increasing the size of the training set
Using a less expressive model
Adding more features to the model

m_Item1.Explanation: Increasing the size of the training dataset helps the model generalize better by providing more examples of each possible data distribution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What technique uses a validation set to tune the hyperparameters and limit overfitting?

Options.OptionText: K-means Clustering
Cross-validation
Gradient Descent
Backpropagation

m_Item1.Explanation: Cross-validation involves partitioning the data into subsets where the training is done on one subset and validated against others. This helps to find the right model complexity and reduce overfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of decision trees, which method is often used to prevent overfitting?

Options.OptionText: Bagging
Boosting
Pruning
Ensembling

m_Item1.Explanation: Pruning is a technique to reduce the size of decision trees by removing sections of the tree that provide little power to classify instances, thus helping to prevent overfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following regularization techniques adds a penalty equal to the square of the magnitude of coefficients?

Options.OptionText: L1 Regularization
Dropout Regularization
Early Stopping
L2 Regularization

m_Item1.Explanation: L2 Regularization, also known as Ridge Regression, adds a penalty equal to the square of the magnitude of coefficients, helping prevent overfitting by discouraging overly complex models.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a form of model averaging that can reduce overfitting?

Options.OptionText: Histogram Binning
Bagging
Normalization
Feature Engineering

m_Item1.Explanation: Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) helps in reducing overfitting by training multiple models and averaging their predictions or allowing them to vote for an outcome.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does it mean if a model performs well on cross-validation but poorly on entirely new data?

Options.OptionText: The model is underfitting
The model is generalizing well
The model parameters are optimized
The validation set closely resembles the training data

m_Item1.Explanation: If a model does well in cross-validation but not on unseen data, the validation set may not be representative of new data due to sampling or it may be too similar to the training set.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does dropout help in reducing overfitting in neural networks?

Options.OptionText: By freezing layers during training
By preventing gradient vanishing
By randomly ignoring neurons during training
By lowering the learning rate

m_Item1.Explanation: Dropout involves randomly setting a fraction of neurons inactive during each training iteration, which helps in making the model less sensitive to specific weights and in reducing overfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements describes overfitting?

Options.OptionText: The model fits the noise in the data
The model fits the general trend in the data
The model does not fit the training data well
The model fits neither training nor test data well

m_Item1.Explanation: Overfitting happens when a model not only learns the underlying patterns in the training data but also fits the noise and random fluctuations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method of validation can help detect overfitting in machine learning models?

Options.OptionText: Using the full dataset as training data
Splitting dataset into training and test sets
Setting a fixed random seed
Using a single validation run

m_Item1.Explanation: Splitting the dataset into separate training and test sets helps ensure that a model does not learn from all available data, allowing its performance to be tested on unseen data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is underfitting in the context of machine learning?

Options.OptionText: When the model performs well on training data but poorly on new data.
When the model is too complex and overlearns noise in the data.
When the model is too simple to capture the underlying pattern of the data.
When the model improves its performance by increasing data dimensions.

m_Item1.Explanation: Underfitting occurs when a model is too simplistic and fails to capture the underlying data patterns accurately, resulting in poor performance on both training and testing data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a typical sign of underfitting?

Options.OptionText: Low training accuracy and low validation accuracy.
High training accuracy and low validation accuracy.
Low training accuracy and high validation accuracy.
High training accuracy and high validation accuracy.

m_Item1.Explanation: In underfitting, both the training and validation accuracies are low because the model fails to capture the structure of the training data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which action is likely to reduce underfitting?

Options.OptionText: Using a simpler model with fewer parameters.
Reducing the training data size.
Increasing the complexity of the model.
Adding more regularization terms.

m_Item1.Explanation: Increasing the model complexity can help in better capturing the data patterns, thereby potentially reducing the risk of underfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Underfitting can be caused by which of the following scenarios?

Options.OptionText: Using a very high learning rate.
Using an overly complex neural network.
Insufficient training data.
Choosing a model that is too rigid.

m_Item1.Explanation: Using a model that is too rigid or simplistic can lead to underfitting as it may not capture the complexity of the data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these models is more prone to underfitting?

Options.OptionText: A deep neural network with many hidden layers.
A high-degree polynomial regression model.
A linear regression model for complex data.
A decision tree with very low depth.

m_Item1.Explanation: A linear regression model might be too simple to fit complex data, making it susceptible to underfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What effect does increasing the number of features in the dataset have on underfitting?

Options.OptionText: It has no effect on underfitting.
It always leads to underfitting.
It potentially reduces underfitting by providing more information to the model.
It increases underfitting due to increased dimensionality.

m_Item1.Explanation: Increasing the number of relevant features can provide more context and information, potentially reducing the risk of underfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following techniques can help avoid underfitting?

Options.OptionText: Decreasing the size of the dataset.
Increasing the number of epochs during training.
Decreasing the model complexity.
Increasing dropout rates.

m_Item1.Explanation: Training the model for more epochs can allow it to learn better representations of the training data, reducing the risk of underfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of polynomial regression, underfitting can be mitigated by:

Options.OptionText: Reducing the degree of the polynomial.
Increasing the degree of the polynomial.
Normalizing the input features.
Regularizing the model more aggressively.

m_Item1.Explanation: Increasing the degree of the polynomial allows the model to fit more complex patterns in the data, thereby reducing underfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scenario illustrates underfitting during training a classification model?

Options.OptionText: The model accurately classifies training data but struggles with test data.
The model achieves less than 60% accuracy on training data.
The model overestimates noise in the test dataset.
The model fits the test data perfectly but not the training data.

m_Item1.Explanation: When a model cannot perform well on the training data itself, such as having low accuracy, it indicates underfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary consequence of underfitting in a real-world machine learning task?

Options.OptionText: The model adapts too closely to the training data, providing little flexibility for new data.
The model behaves similarly across training and new data, often too simplistic.
The model captures noise along with data patterns, reducing its generalization ability.
The model uses excessive computational resources.

m_Item1.Explanation: Underfitting yields a model that performs similarly on training and unseen data, often due to being overly simplistic and failing to capture the underlying patterns.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of the bias-variance trade-off, which of the following does high bias in a model typically indicate?

Options.OptionText: The model is likely overfitting the training data.
The model is likely underfitting the training data.
The model has a high variance in its predictions.
The model is performing perfectly.

m_Item1.Explanation: High bias indicates that the model is making strong assumptions about the data, often leading to underfitting because it doesn't capture the complexity of the data well.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true regarding the bias-variance trade-off?

Options.OptionText: Increasing model complexity always reduces variance but increases bias.
Decreasing model complexity always reduces bias but increases variance.
Increasing model complexity reduces bias but can increase variance.
Decreasing model complexity increases both bias and variance.

m_Item1.Explanation: Increasing model complexity tends to reduce bias because a more complex model can capture more patterns, but it may increase variance because the model can fit the noise in the data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary goal when addressing the bias-variance trade-off?

Options.OptionText: To minimize model complexity at all costs.
To achieve the highest possible accuracy on training data.
To find a balance that minimizes error on unseen data.
To ensure the model follows a linear hypothesis.

m_Item1.Explanation: The primary goal is to find a balance between bias and variance that results in the lowest possible generalization error, ensuring good performance on unseen data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scenario indicates a perfect balance in the bias-variance trade-off?

Options.OptionText: Both bias and variance are minimized to zero.
Bias errors are high, and variance errors are low.
Bias remains constant while variance tends infinitely high.
The sum of bias and variance errors is minimized.

m_Item1.Explanation: A perfect balance in the bias-variance trade-off achieves the minimum total error by balancing both bias and variance, rather than trying to minimize each independently.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What impact does increasing the size of the training dataset generally have on the bias-variance trade-off?

Options.OptionText: It decreases both bias and variance.
It typically increases bias but reduces variance.
It stabilizes variance but leaves bias unchanged.
It typically reduces variance without affecting bias significantly.

m_Item1.Explanation: Increasing the size of the training dataset generally provides more examples, which reduces variance because the model can learn more about the data structure without significantly changing existing biases.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Assume we have a dataset that can be trained with 100 percent accuracy using a decision tree of depth 6. Consider the following points and select an option based on them. Note : All other hyperparameters are the same, and no other factors are impacted.
(1) Depth 4 has a strong bias and a low variance.
(2) Depth 4 will be low in bias and variance.

Options.OptionText: Only 1
Only 2
Only 1 and 2
None of the above

m_Item1.Explanation: It will result in underfit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following assertions about bias and variance is true?

Options.OptionText: Models which overfit have a low variance.
Models which overfit have a low bias
Models which underfit have a high variance
Models which underfit have a low variance.

m_Item1.Explanation:
Models which underfit have a low variance.
​
​
​

m_Item1.QuestionText: Regarding bias and variance, which of the follwing statements are true?

Options.OptionText: Models which overfit have a high bias and underfit have a high variance
Models which overfit have a high bias and underfit have a low variance
Models which overfit have a low bias and underfit have a high variance
Models which overfit have a low bias and underfit have a low variance

m_Item1.Explanation: Models which overfit have a low bias and underfit have a low variance

m_Item1.QuestionText: While fitting a linear regression to the data, you see the following: As the amount of training data grows, the test error reduces but the training error increases. The train error is pretty low (nearly as low as you would anticipate), while the test error is significantly greater than the train error. What do you believe is the primary cause for this behavior? Select the most likely option.

Options.OptionText: High variance
High model bias
High estimation bias
None of the above

m_Item1.Explanation: When the training error is less than test error then it is condition of overfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following methods is not typically used to prevent overfitting in machine learning models?

Options.OptionText: Dropout regularization.
Early stopping.
Increasing model complexity.
Cross-validation.

m_Item1.Explanation: By increasing model complexity, it may lead to underfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens to a model's bias and variance as it becomes more complex?

Options.OptionText: Bias decreases and variance increases.
Bias increases and variance decreases.
Both bias and variance increase.
Both bias and variance decrease.

m_Item1.Explanation: Increasing model complexity tends to decrease bias (because the model can better fit the training data) but may increase variance (because the model becomes more sensitive to noise and specific characteristics of the training data).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which technique helps in detecting overfitting by splitting the dataset into multiple subsets for training and validation?

Options.OptionText: Dropout.
Regularization.
Cross-validation.
Batch normalization.

m_Item1.Explanation: Cross validation is a sampling technique which is used to address the problem of  overfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which scenario would regularization be most useful?

Options.OptionText: When the model is underfitting.
When the model is overfitting.
When the model has high bias.
When the model has high variance.

m_Item1.Explanation: Regularization techniques (L1, L2 or L1L2) are more suitable in case of overfitting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a common indicator of underfitting when analyzing learning curves?

Options.OptionText: Large gap between training and validation error.
Small gap between training and validation error.
Decreasing training error and increasing validation error.
Increasing training error and decreasing validation error.

m_Item1.Explanation: This pattern suggests that the model is not fitting the training data well (high bias or underfitting), as indicated by the continuing decrease in training error (indicating the model can still improve on the training data) and the increasing validation error (indicating the model is not generalizing well to unseen data).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a measure of how well a machine learning model generalizes to new, unseen data?

Options.OptionText: Training accuracy.
Test accuracy.
Both Trainig and Testing accuracy
None of the above.

m_Item1.Explanation: Testing accuracy is the measure of how well a machine learning model generalizes to new, unseen data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _______ is the scenario when the model fails to decipher the underlying trend in the input data.

Options.OptionText: Overfitting
Underfitting
Both A and B
None of the above

m_Item1.Explanation: Underfitting is the scenario when the model fails to decipher the underlying trend in the input data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a common approach to reducing overfitting in deep learning?

Options.OptionText: Dropout
Batch normalization
Early stopping
All of the above

m_Item1.Explanation: Overfitting is a common problem in deep learning, where the model becomes too complex and memorizes the training data instead of learning generalizable patterns.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Residuals are useful for investigating best model fit.

Options.OptionText: True
False

m_Item1.Explanation: Yes, residuals are useful for investigating best fit model.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following data sets provides the most realistic estimate of the performance of a model on completely unseen data?

Options.OptionText: training data set
linear regression data set
test data set
none of the above

m_Item1.Explanation: Test data set provides the most realistic estimate of the performance of a model on completely unseen data.
